did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (CNNs), Swarm Intelligence (Grasshopper Optimization Algorithm for segmentation), and feature extraction (SURF) to perform skin lesion segmentation and melanoma classification using dermoscopy images from ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, and PH-2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid Grasshopper optimization algorithm for skin lesion segmentation and melanoma classification. It details specific methods (Swarm Intelligence, SURF, CNN), datasets used (ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, PH-2), and reports new empirical results including accuracy (98.52%), precision, and MCC, comparing them against state-of-the-art methods.

title: A hybrid Grasshopper optimization algorithm for skin lesion segmentation and melanoma classification using deep learning
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid approach for skin lesion segmentation and melanoma classification using dermoscopy images from the ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, and PH-2 datasets. The method employs Swarm Intelligence via the Grasshopper Optimization Algorithm for lesion segmentation and Speeded-Up Robust Features (SURF) for feature extraction. These features are subsequently classified using deep learning techniques, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The proposed methodology was evaluated against state-of-the-art methods using metrics including accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F-measure, Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), Dice coefficient, and Jaccard index. The results demonstrated superior performance, achieving an accuracy of 98.52%, precision of 96.73%, and MCC of 97.04%. The authors conclude that their suggested classification and segmentation methodologies outperform existing approaches across all evaluated performance indicators.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for classification in the study. The paper focuses on distinguishing melanoma from non-melanoma lesions using datasets ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, and PH-2. The PH-2 dataset specifically includes 'Atypical Nevi' and 'Ordinary Nevi' alongside melanoma.

Used as the comparator class to melanoma in the binary classification task (benign vs. malignant). The paper mentions Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) as prevalent non-melanoma malignancies in the introduction but does not explicitly state they were separate classes in the final experimental classification model, which focused on melanoma vs. non-melanoma.

Mentioned in the introduction as one of the most prevalent skin malignancies and a type of non-melanoma cancer, but not explicitly listed as a distinct experimental class in the segmentation/classification results.

Mentioned in the introduction as a type of non-melanoma cancer that can cause scaly red patches and bleeding, but not explicitly listed as a distinct experimental class in the segmentation/classification results.
Included in the PH-2 reference dataset used to evaluate the system technique, along with Ordinary Nevi and melanoma.
Included in the PH-2 reference dataset used to evaluate the system technique, along with Atypical Nevi and melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion regions (Region of Interest/ROI) in dermoscopy images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a hybrid Grasshopper Optimization Algorithm (GOA) for skin lesion segmentation to locate lesions on skin areas. Performance is evaluated using Dice coefficient and Jaccard index.

The paper performs melanoma classification using features extracted via SURF and classified using CNNs. The task involves distinguishing between benign and malignant tumors (binary classification) and potentially multi-class classification (e.g., using PH-2 dataset with Atypical Nevi, Ordinary Nevi, and Melanoma). Metrics include accuracy (98.52%), precision, sensitivity, specificity, and MCC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2017
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 dataset, specifically the 'Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection' task. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The paper notes it includes over 10,000 shots in the full collection, but the study utilized a subset.

use.training: Used for training the classification model (60% of the 1000 images used in the study, i.e., 600 images per class).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification model (40% of the 1000 images used in the study, i.e., 400 images per class).
use.other: Used for segmentation evaluation (Dice coefficient and Jaccard index).

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2018
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 dataset, comprising dermoscopic images of skin lesions and melanoma detection analyses. The study utilized a subset of 1000 images.

use.training: Used for training the classification model (60% of the 1000 images used in the study).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification model (40% of the 1000 images used in the study).

datasets_used.name: PH-2
datasets_used.description: A database containing dermoscopic pictures of skin lesions, including images of actual skin lesion segmentation for experimental analysis. The study utilized 600 images from this dataset.

use.training: Used for training the classification model (60% of the 600 images used in the study, i.e., 120 images per class).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification model (40% of the 600 images used in the study, i.e., 80 images per class).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, sensitivity, specificity, F-measure, MCC, Dice coefficient, and Jaccard index, with claims of superiority over state-of-the-art methods based on these metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

ISIC-2017.notes: The paper states that the ISIC-2017 dataset provided the highest standard MCC of 97.04% and a greater dice coefficient compared to other datasets. The average specificity across all three datasets was 0.9872.

ISIC-2018.notes: The intended approach had greater average specificity when using the ISIC-2018 dataset compared to the other two datasets. The MCC was lower than that of ISIC-2017.

PH-2.notes: The MCC was greater when using the PH-2 dataset compared to the ISIC-2018 dataset. The PH-2 dataset yielded an average accuracy of 0.9710 for precision.

overall_summary.notes: The abstract reports an estimated accuracy of 98.52%, preciseness of 96.73%, and MCC of 97.04%. The results section details specific per-dataset accuracies of 98.42% (ISIC-2017), 98.19% (ISIC-2018), and 98.01% (PH-2). The method outperformed state-of-the-art methods in every performance indicator.

architecture_details.layers: Three convolutional layers followed by maxpooling and fully connected layers
architecture_details.activation_functions: Sigmoid functions for the network's fully linked layers; nonlinear activation functions used for the fully linked layers
architecture_details.feature_input: Optimized SURF (Speeded-Up Robust Features) feature pattern of Region of Interest (ROL)

training_details.optimizer: Grid search approach used to optimize hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, dropout rate)
training_details.epochs: Initialized but specific number not reported
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i7-10700K CPU, 32 GB DDR4 RAM, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 Ti GPU

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using HR-IQE (Hair Removal and Image Quality Enhancement) technique
Intensity centered image quality improvement to enhance individual pixels in hair-free dermoscopy images
Noise elimination from skin lesion (SL) images
conclusions: The proposed hybrid approach combining Swarm Intelligence (Grasshopper Optimization Algorithm) for segmentation and Speeded-Up Robust Features (SURF) with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for classification outperformed state-of-the-art methods across all evaluated performance indicators.
The method achieved high classification efficacy, with an estimated accuracy of 98.52%, precision of 96.73%, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 97.04%.
The Grasshopper Optimization technique produced the best segmentation outcomes for identifying the Region of Interest (ROI) in dermoscopy images.

The suggested classification and segmentation methodologies improve specialists’ ability to distinguish benign from malignant tumors, potentially aiding in early detection and reducing mortality rates.
The study demonstrates the effectiveness of using dermoscopy images and AI techniques to address the challenges of skin cancer diagnosis, such as fuzzy borders and background noise.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying exclusively on three public datasets (ISIC-2017, ISIC-2018, PH-2) without testing on independent clinical cohorts.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over state-of-the-art baselines.
The paper does not report specificity values for individual datasets (ISIC-2018, PH-2) or MCC for ISIC-2018 and PH-2, creating gaps in the reported performance metrics.
The classification task is binary (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma), limiting clinical applicability for distinguishing specific non-melanoma subtypes like Basal Cell Carcinoma or Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
The PH-2 dataset used for evaluation is small (600 images total), which may limit the generalizability of the results for that specific subset.
The study claims outperformance over state-of-the-art methods but does not provide detailed comparative metrics for all baselines across all datasets in the provided context.

The methodology uses a hybrid approach (GOA for segmentation, SURF for feature extraction, CNN for classification) which may introduce complexity and computational overhead compared to end-to-end deep learning models.
The paper does not address potential data leakage risks or the specific preprocessing steps applied to the ISIC datasets beyond resizing and normalization, which are critical for reproducibility.
